config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. Society’s interests matter, but they should not automatically override individual rights, because once that line is crossed, it becomes too easy for governments or majorities to justify abuse. In my view, the best society is one that protects people’s basic rights first, while allowing limits only when they are necessary, fair, and clearly defined. From my perspective, collective goals are important, but they should never become a blank check to sacrifice the individual.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The rights of the individual should generally come first, because society is strongest when it protects people from being trampled by the majority or the government. From a United States perspective, I am very wary of any claim that “society” should automatically override individual rights, because history shows how easily that language can justify censorship, discrimination, or abuse. That said, I do not believe individual rights are absolute in every case; public safety, fraud prevention, and basic laws can justify limits when they are narrowly tailored and clearly necessary. So I would reject the statement as written: society matters, but it should not take priority over rights by default.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to “the common good” can be used to pressure people into silence, conformity, or unfair treatment, especially when power is uneven. Society absolutely needs shared rules, but those rules should protect human dignity first and only limit rights when there is a clear, narrow, and accountable reason. If we reverse that priority, it becomes too easy to excuse abuse in the name of harmony.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over individual rights as a general rule. From a United States perspective, I do not trust vague appeals to “the common good” when they can so easily become cover for censorship, discrimination, or government overreach, and our history gives plenty of reasons to be cautious. At the same time, I do not see rights as unlimited in every situation, because laws for public safety and preventing harm are legitimate when they are narrow and clearly justified. So I would put the burden on society to prove a specific, necessary reason before limiting rights, not assume society wins by default.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how “society” and “harmony” can be used to pressure people into silence or unfair treatment, especially when the people in power decide what counts as the common good. I do think some limits on rights are necessary for public safety and order, but those limits must be narrow, accountable, and based on clear harm, not just majority preference. If society is allowed to outrank individual rights by default, it becomes far too easy to excuse abuse in the name of unity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over individual rights by default. As someone from the United States, I am especially suspicious of vague appeals to the “common good,” because they have often been used to justify censorship, discrimination, and government overreach. I do think society can impose limited rules for public safety and preventing real harm, but the burden should always be on society to prove that the restriction is necessary, narrow, and accountable. If we reverse the priority, we make it too easy for majorities to trample people who are unpopular, vulnerable, or simply in the way.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
